speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Nison Katz, tape number five. October 2nd, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I-- I was saying, so I've seen the three couples, and they told me, if you want to know where we used to live-- I mean, they didn't stay in one place all the time. They moved around because they were afraid. If you want to know where we lived, lately, till we got-- you-- you-- the-- the-- the war ended, and you came over here, I'll show you. They took us on a field and told us, here is the place we lived. Find-- find-- find this place. We had been looking around. We couldn't find it. You couldn't. And then they said, move over a little bit. And I moved over. They opened up a bunch of grass, and there was a house. A hole, in the underground. And I mean, they made from-- from the ground. They dig it out like a bed. They had a-- a oven, like, from-- not-- not steel, just something like that. They could heat it up with-- with food over something. It was there, like, potatoes, some roasted potatoes, some-- some carrots, what-- some-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] something like-- like this. But with them, they lived all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went back to our camp. When we got there, we had-- our major was a Jew. So we told him what we saw, what we-- we talked to them. So we took him, and we went at the same place. He couldn't find it neither.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, they told him everything, what is-- how they got-- they lived over the war. The-- what-- and he took them onto the city. He got for them some houses, and he provided them with food, in the beginning. Then we-- we left. I mean, we couldn't stay with them. But I mean, just fact. How people survived at that time. Then-- then-- the-- the United State what had a part of Germany there. They moved in. I mean, it was still half Russian, half Germans. And the-- the US camp, there was a lot of them, a lot of people they took in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people-- three girls there was wandering on the street. And we have seen these are Jewish girls, and we started to talk to them. And they told us, one was from Holland, one was from Romania, and the third one I-- I-- I don't recall that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were sort-- they hardly could walk. They've been working at German's factories there in Germany. When started the war, some people were in the ghetto. Some people got killed. And they were young kids. They send them out to Germany to work-- to work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so when the war was finished, they got out there. So we took them in. And we got some houses. There was enough there. We didn't have to fight for that. But-- and we got them food. And we asked them where they want to go, what they want to do. If they want to go to-- I mean, to Holland, to Romania, all right? Because it still wasn't other Russians there, Czech, and Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: They says, no, we don't want to go there. We are going to go-- we want to go to the States. So we took them in-- in the camp, in-- in the US camps there. So we took them on the-- I mean, I couldn't take them. Well, we had the major, a Jewish major. He got a truck. And at night, we took them out, and we put them there on the-- on the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Katz, have you told your story to your kids or your grandchildren, at least?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. They-- they taped it but not all. They didn't finish. But they did. They have it, a tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you teach your grandchildren about this? Is there anything you think we can learn from all your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think they can and they do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can-- what do they-- what can they learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, they-- they-- first of all, the most important thing, what they understand, that they are Jews. Don't say wherever you live, and how good you are, it's comes to a point-- it's comes to a point that they point you out you are a Jew. Because we lived it, too. German Jews, the same thing. They thought it won't get to them. When Hitler started in '39-- when he started it, they-- they laughed at-- at him. They thought it was crazy that he made a revolution. It started in 1933. '39, when the war started, the-- the Polish Army took-- took some Jews, soldiers. We were Polish citizens. They took us to the-- to the Army and they send us to the front, not me, but I mean, the people. Some-- I mean, some people got killed. Some people went to Germany as [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] war vet, not war veterans. Like in-- because-- like-- in plan-- like in-- you-- you-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] like I was a-- a soldier, shall we say, and the German soldiers, what took you like in-- a prisoner. Some was prisoners there. And we received letters. I'm talking now from our city. It was from our city, too. We received letters what they are doing to them, being in Poland, before the war. So, well, people in Poland, the same thing-- thought they are crazy. It won't happen to me, but it did, to everyone. This is the most important thing for what they learn. What I told them. To be aware that he's a Jew. Don't forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But just because you're aware, so how-- so what does that do?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, what? If you're aware, is that all you should do, is just be aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or is there something more you should do?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, at that time, we weren't aware because it wasn't a Jewish state. Now it's different. Now we have [INAUDIBLE]. They've got to fight for it, and they do. And they do. That's what they know. And I mean, like, my son and my daughter-in-law, they are the same thing. I mean, they know. I mean, they have-- in Israel, my daughter-in-law has aunts and relatives, a lot of them. Some of them are in Moshavot, in Israel. They coming up here. We're going there. So they know. This is the most important thing, to me, at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's important to support Israel, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and to know you're Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --even if you live outside of Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the main thing. Wherever you-- you are, don't forget. It's good 100%. 100%-- it can't be 100%-- but you think it's good. But then it comes to a point, they point you out. You are a Jew. It's better not to come to it. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How can you prevent that from happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how-- how can we prevent that? Now that we have our own country, we got to support it, even if we are here. We are here, but we can-- we can and we should and we must help them. They are dying then. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We are alive now. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you want to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's too much already. I talk too much already. But still can talk without-- without stoppage another two hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything important that you didn't-- that you didn't say yet, that you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I did the most-- the most of the things I-- I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Katz, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. I am in the back row, the shortest--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right of the shortest man.

speaker: interviewee
text: To right on the shortest man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just below the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: Below the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And where is this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Ostrog. In [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the Zionist youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's it, the Zionist youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and it's 1932.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1932, Right. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At the photograph there, I am at the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right. On the right photograph, you're on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, it was after the war in Germany, it was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but you're a Russian soldier there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was a Russian soldier. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where exactly was it taken in Germany? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: At-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it was taken after I entered the war in the Army. It was [GERMAN], it was taken. It was [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the-- what was the reason for the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, it was the end of the war. And we knew that sooner or later, we're going to be demobilized. So for to remember each other, we took a picture. It was a friend of ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what about the picture on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the picture on the left, I am in the right side, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the top.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the top. And this also was taken at the same-- at the same time what-- and they're friends, from the Russian Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of any of these other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I'm there in the right, in the bottom row.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the second row.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the second row on the right side. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture been taken in Ostrog on the-- on the Brother's [INAUDIBLE] there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: The cemetery. It's not a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the occasion for this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The occasion was to remember [INTERPOSING VOICES] because it was exactly before we should left-- leave, I mean, Russia to Poland, to have to remember our loved ones, where Hitler's Germans killed them. This is a mass grave for people from our-- from our city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm there on the right side. With me in the middle is my niece, Zora. In the left side, her husband, [? Simcah ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Ostrog, in 1946, [INAUDIBLE] when I came to Ostrog to visit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's niece-- whose daughter is Zora?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zora is my brother-in-law's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your brother, who? Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I-- I-- I-- I-- that's what I know. It was an-- an older brother, a stepbrother. Not really-- so I don't remember him but that's what I've been told. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that picture, I'm with my wife on the right side, with my two sons, Tevye and [? Grisha. ?] In the middle is my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sister-in-law. In the left side--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Tova. ?] In the left side is my niece with her husband and the two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tom and [? Myrtle. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the-- what are the niece and nephew's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Sula ?] and [? Simcah. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1957. It's in 1958.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, I guess it's in Liegnitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the occasion for this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo the occasion was they lived in another city, and we lived in another city. So before leaving to Canada, we took the picture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my birth certificate. It has been taken-- it's a Russian birth certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what it says on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I had the glasses I could. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] Oh, that's a bit better. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is said, [RUSSIAN] Katz, Nison [INAUDIBLE] December 10, 1916. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my passport, Polish passport. It-- It's been given to me on Warsaw. And it was in 1950s-- I don't remember. It's, I guess, '57 or-- or something. Can I have a look?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our marriage certificate. It's been taken-- we got married in Rokitna, in 1946. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In this picture is my oldest son, Tom, his wife, Elaine, his daughter, Hailey, and Jordan, this is bar mitzvah boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] But he's the younger-- he's the younger son's--

speaker: interviewee
text: No this is-- this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: The other son's boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other son's, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was three years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Shaar Shalom.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Toronto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Thornhill.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In this picture, is from the right, my oldest grandson, Ari, my daughter-in-law, Sandra, Jordan, the bar mitzvah boy, my son, the youngest son, [? Harold, ?] and the baby, JD, Jonathan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- where and when was this taken? Also two years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Ethel Gojcherman. OK? We got married in May 31st, 1946. We have currently in our family, from two sons, two-- two daughter-in-laws, four grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've known each other how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we've been know each other since we were little ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: How little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, seven, eight, nine years old. We used to live on the same street. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't play together, but we knew each other. Till about 1936 or '35, we start more-- more or less to go together. And during the war, we've been separated. I mean, we-- we hadn't been married. And we got married in '46 and thank God, till now. No complaints. [LAUGHTER]
